Hey, how's it going?
Good.
How are you?
Good.
Good.

That's okay.
I noticed that.
I was like, is he here yet?
I know I'm running a little late.
Maybe I'm okay.

I really don't know its logic in picking which one is the default account that it selects, but it always seems to confound what I actually want it to be.
Yeah, I think that's the default logic.
Whichever one you want is the one it doesn't pick.
Exactly.
Thanks for doing this.
I really appreciate it.
My pleasure.
Yeah, it's lovely to talk more about Aembit and kind of your role there and also what you think is missing from the content piece as well and how we can...
And improve that.

But I would love just a bit of background on you and on, you know, how you came to Aembit and how you got into technology to begin with and stuff like that would be super interesting to me if you're willing to kick it off with that.
As you know, my name is John Redmon.
I've been in the technology field professionally for about 20 years.
Graduated with a computer science degree and pretty much been doing it ever since.
Really started as a software developer.
That's kind where my passion lies.
But at my last job, I spent a couple of years as head of DevOps and then went back to software development.
So now I kind of, one of the few developers that can live in both worlds between DevOps and software engineering.
For 10 years before this job, I worked for, originally for a company called Republic Parking.
It was a parking company.
They were bought out by Imperial Parking out of Canada, which was then bought out by Reef Technology.
Oh, yeah, I'm familiar.
Okay.
And so as part of that job, our CSUN contacted me and said, there's this startup that's really getting off the ground.
They're called Aembit.
And was wondering if you'd talk with them and, you know, kind of give them a debug bed for the software and kind of help them progress and see how that goes.
So that's how I originally met the team.
I was probably one of the first external users, not going to claim the title of first, but I was one of the first.
You're in there, yeah.
Yeah, to use the product.
And we had a lot of positive feedback back and forth.
And I gave them suggestions on, you know, the features that had next, and they would, you know, iterate and we would debug through it.
And I had a debug cluster.
That housed a copy of our entire infrastructure.
So I would house it there and keep track of it and actually ran it in our prod cluster for a long time.
And after, I guess, about two and a half years of knowing them and working with them, they just sort of quietly under the table said, are you interested in a move?
And I was like, you know, for most people, I would say no.
But for you guys, I'd be happy to.
So we talked and I jumped ship last December.
I'd be here ever since.
Nice.
Yeah, the customer to employee story is always a good one, right?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I mean, I think, you know, if you work with somebody and you have enough faith in what they're doing and the people doing it to say, yes, I will come join you.
I mean, I think that's probably the biggest vote of confidence you can give a company, you know?
Absolutely.
Yeah.
I don't know if you had any interaction with like our team early on in the engagement, but Kyle, who does a
A lot of the early stand-up stuff and initial onboarding, that was his story, too, of how he came to us.
was like Marcel's first customer, and then through that process, he was like, you know, I'm going to join.
Yeah, I want to be on site, so it's cool.

And I have to say, that's one of the things I really find fascinating about this company is like almost everybody here is either a friend of one of the originals or they used to work for a customer and they came over.
Yes.

So I think that's really inspiring how, you know, the team itself has the dynamic and the outward-facing appearance that this is a team I want to be a part of, not to mention a technology that everybody that engages with it is like, this is really awesome.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I talked to Victor earlier, and he's been around working with the founders for 20 years, he said, right?
So that's huge, especially through multiple different companies, different outcomes.
Like, you don't find that kind of...
Yeah, core team of the, you know, that started...
The company, mean, they've worked together off and on for, you know, some of them even longer than that.
I mean, they met years ago and they've just been moving around together.
And I mean, that kind of dedication to each other is almost unheard of.
Yeah.
Amazing.
I love that.
Yeah, it's really good.
I've been covering startups because I was a journalist before and like writing about them for basically 20 years.
And like, you don't hear that story that much, right?
Like, not something that comes up.
Yeah.
I mean, I know people that used to work together that are still friends.
Yeah.
I don't know a lot of people that have moved as a unit, you know, one leaves and the other follow.
Yeah.
And there's only been one or two people in my career that if they'd have called me and said, Hey, I need you here.
I've been like, I'm on my way.
Yeah.
And that's actually how I accepted not this job, but the last job.
Nice.
A really good friend of mine called me and I was doing contract work for a match.
So,
He's like, I really need your help.
And I'm like, you need my help?
He's like, yeah, it's bad.
I need your help.
I said, okay, set up the interview.
So I drove from Arkansas back to Tennessee and did the interview.
And he said, all right, here's the deal.
I said, what's that?
He's like, I'm going to hire you pending a background check.
But the deal is, because it happened on a Friday, the deal is I need you to start two weeks from Monday, regardless.
So I went back to work the next Monday and said, guys, I hate to bring it to you.
This is my two-week notice.
I may not have a job in two weeks, but this is what we're going with.
So here we go.
Nice.
Well, I mean, yeah, you can do that without a net for very specific people.
Yeah, for most people, not a chance.
I got to go.
Yeah.
I mean, I kind of ended up here saying my old boss, Matthew, who is our chief content officer.
He was like, he just called me one day and he was like, you bored?
And I was like, why are you asking?
An odd way to start a conversation.
What's going on?
Yeah, well, it's worked out.
That's cool.
When you were a customer, was there something about the product that really attracted you, too?
Or was it like a combination team product?
It was the product that got me first because of what it accomplished.
Because in our Kubernetes clusters, we were running Spring Boot, Java.
And all of our secrets within our code for all of our passwords.
so being injected by this Spring Boot configuration manager, which, you know, unfortunately, thankfully, we actually had a vault attached to it.
So some of it was really, you know, secure, but some of it was kind of eh.

But, you know, if you've ever worked with it before, you know that even if it is secure from the source, every time the program starts, it reads those in, and every variable it reads in becomes an environment variable.
So if your environment's ever compromised, all that stuff's just sitting there.
And Aembit was like, what if you didn't have to do that anymore?
What if you could set it up in Aembit and then it would infect it in real time based on the request and you never had to touch it again?
And I was like, that would be amazing.
And so that's what really got my attention.

And then, you know, working with them as an outsider, but yet as welcoming as they were to me in that process, that iterative process, you know, I was giving, you know, and I actually, even in the beginning, gave them some ideas and tools that I was using because at that time I was running DevOps, you know, and there were some tools I had.
They're like, what is that?
That's really cool.
You know, and we kind of went through this whole thing.
And, and I think that was just, just really eye-opening that there were people out there like that, that not only, you know, they wanted the help, but they were engaging.
So that, you know, in a weird way, even then I felt like I was a part of their team.
Yeah, that's

Get iterations about, you know, changes they would make or do interfaces they had, you know, and I even talked to their graphics designers, even when I was, you know, their first iteration of the rebuild.
And I was, like, giving them my thoughts and critiques, and I'm just like, this is awesome.
Nice.
Yeah, people who are open to feedback, especially about their particular product, even when they're, like, the ones providing it to you, like, that's always a good sign, too, right?
Oh, absolutely.
That means that they care more about the end product than they do about any particular engagement.
Exactly.
Yeah, exactly.

I'm curious, too, like, when you talk about, like, what you found such or so attractive about the product, like, do you think that, this is something I talked to Victor about, too, which was just kind of, like, what do you wish was better communicated outside of the company and to whom?
And, like, where do you see gaps there, or where do you see more?
Our opportunities, even if it's already kind of being done well, but we could like do more volume on that.
Like, do you have any thoughts on that?

So, you know, watching, you know, sort of the zeitgeist over the last year, you know, the key terms that matter to us, like NHI, you know, access management and these sort of things, they're becoming more frequent.
You know, more people are using them.
I'm not sure that everybody completely understands what that means yet, but we're moving in that direction.

You know, I would like to see, you know, some more, because I think, and I mean, this is just me thinking, which, you know, I've been told I've never been good at, you know, that if people truly understood what that meant, like what we provide and what it's doing, I think a lot more people would be like, I need to be on board with that.
Because I was having a conversation with somebody and I was like, okay, think about it this way.
They're like, okay, so like, you're like the head of HR of a company.
And or the head of security for a company and a new guy gets hired.
They're like, okay.
I'm like, you just give that guy admin credentials and say, go get after it.
And they're like, of course it's not.
That's stupid.

I'm like, well, what you don't realize is that anything you give your applications access to, even if it's deployed within, I mean, these are potential vulnerabilities that can be taken from you, or you could have a man in the middle, or you could have a bad actor.
And I'm like, you know, what we do is you protect your employees.
We protect your components, whether it be software pieces or the devices, you know, and I'm like, it's good that people are really starting to think about that now.
And I think, you know, more people need to view these sort of things as that needs to be protected, just like my employee does.
Yeah, that's what I wonder about with the, like, specifically with you guys is like, is there, is it fair to do?
A real equivalency on, no, like, it is, like, one-to-one with people access management.
Like, everything you're thinking about with people access management, you should be thinking about over here.
Because I think what's poorly understood is, like, people are like, yeah, but, like, a person is so susceptible to, like, let's say, social engineering or whatever.
But, like, a machine is not.
Like, a machine is a good instruction follower.

So, therefore, I don't have to worry about these things in the same way I do a person who is, who has free will and is, therefore, subject to, like, all kinds of randomness variables or unpredictable variables that, like, a machine, which I, it's cold logic.
Like, I can understand that, right?
So, I feel like there's a do understand that argument a little bit.
But, you my response to that is usually, yes, I understand what you're saying.
And as long as everything is tightly boxed, you know, you're really bringing the margin of there being a leak, you know, very minuscule numbers.
But you have to remember that your database...
It's not really checking the ID badges of whoever knocks at the door.
If they've got the password, they're just swinging the door opens and come on in.
Now, what we do is not only do we provide those secret injections, but we actually validate the source.

And we have multiple different ways we can do that, whether it be based on the AWS account it's running in, whether it be the Kubernetes cluster it's in, whether it be the namespace the pods are running in, the name of the pod.
You know, we can drill that down so that when that request gets made, we validate, yes, this person should actually have access to this credential, and then we inject it.
Whereas even if a bad actor got in your cluster and they spawned a pod, our system would just ignore it because it doesn't match your criteria.
So, yes, in some ways, as long as you type that security around your application, you can definitely get those numbers down, not to zero, but down.
But we also.
Provide the benefit of being the gatekeeper for those other systems that the only gatekeeper is the password.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, I mean, that also has the equivalent in a human, right?
Because you do the access profile, too.
Like, where are they calling from and what, you know, what time of day is it and that kind of stuff.
You know, and a lot of people just focus on the secret management.
And I'm like, yeah, that's a piece of it.
But we offer the other pieces, too, because it's closer to one-to-one than people think.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah, feel like that might help.
Because I think you're right that people, like, there's such a broad audience that would benefit from a better understanding.
And they're maybe just, like, unaware that it even would impact them in any way.
But I think especially, too, because you get, I think organizations are looking for efficiencies.
And one of the ways they're doing it is by, like, assigning what used to be considered highly technical tasks to non-technical managers or buyers or whatever, right?
And you're like, right.
Well, do they understand everything that's going on?
But then the other problem that Victor articulated is, like, let's say you have a CISO who does kind of get it, at least from, like, a big picture.
But then the implementation engineer might be resistant, too, because they don't understand it at a granular level.
They're overwhelmed or they're like, we're fine with HashiCorp or whatever else they have going on, right?
Like, they don't want to take the time to then do the actual integration.
And that blocks a lot of your, like, uptake, even, like, post-purchase or whatever, right?

You know, and I have found that to be one of the unique constraints of this product is that, you know, it's a situation where the team that actually benefits, which is usually security, is usually not the team that will be responsible for implementing and maintaining it.
So, you know, you have usually DevOps and SecOps sitting there.
And my personal experience...
I hope it's not universal, but my personal experience is those two groups don't always get along because, you know, SecOps is always trying to strangle DevOps and DevOps wants to break free.
So when SecOps comes and says, here's another package, I don't know anything about how it works, but I need it in the system tomorrow.
You know, DevOps is just ready to punch somebody and then nothing goes the way it should, you know.

And that's one thing that we have been trying to approach is that, you know, not just approaching SecOps, approaching DevOps, approaching developers, letting them know where those benefits come in, what we can provide for them so that, you know, when it does get up the food chain, they already have that understanding of this is the goal we're trying to get to.
This is how you can help us so that when they come back down and say, hey, we want to implement this.
They're not like, what is that?
They'd like, oh, yeah, that's a good idea.
Let's do that.
Yeah, yeah.
That's he was talking a lot about that, like raising awareness in those circles.
So when you come in, it's not.
Unknown quantity.
You're like, oh, I've heard of them.
They say smart stuff.
They do smart work in this or whatever, right?
So like that is well heard.
And then that was the thing that we were trying to count right on to.
How do we talk to the audience with our specific tooling where we've done a lot to fix it?
Because like we had to fight its nature a bit early on.
that was why we had some difficulties because its nature was to provide SEO optimized marketing content, right?
I was kind of like hard coded in a lot of the products that we had built.

And what's great is now that we, this is maybe too much context, but like we were on the, we were on Aerofs as a rail, like they were doing a lot of linkages behind and then we took that over ourselves.
And we got a lot more flexibility in terms of how we make the calls and like what information we provide along with the calls to the various models and then how we bring that back and synthesize it.
So we've been able to.
Be more targeted for different specific needs, right?

So if you want to do like a more educational need to a highly technical audience who's looking for practical stuff, like we can now do that without having to just kind of like try to layer it on top and then fight against its own innate programming interaction.
Yeah, that's awesome.

Yeah, because that was another thing we've talked about is that, you know, when you're dealing with the structure tree, you know, when you get high enough up the structure, you know, they don't necessarily want to know how it works.
They just want to know, what's this going to do for me?
Is this going to help my bottom line?
And then you start getting down the ladder and it gets more technical the further down you go.
Yeah.
You know, and that's the other thing about it.
It's like, you know, finding the people that can, and that's exactly what you said.
You know, it's not now just trying to have something technical that you have to try to cram on top of your generalized offering.
You can now tailor it the way you want it.
I think that's great.
Yeah.
Yeah, we should be able to serve all, right?
And then the.
This is where they're going to find it, as opposed to hoping that this broad content will appeal in some small way to each, from the same piece, which I think is a lot of how it used to work before.
You would be like, yeah, let's consider all these audiences.
you're like, that doesn't work, right?
Consider all, serve none.
That's the way it works, right?
But yeah, I'm also curious, too, what does your day-to-day look like, I guess, in Aembit?
Like, are you doing a lot of customer-facing stuff, or are you more on the internal tooling and architecture and stuff, or what?
So, me personally, I work on the cloud team, so a lot of my stuff actually does end up either being customer-facing or right behind it.
I prefer to be right behind it.
Like I told my boss, you know, my UI skills, I can do it, but it looks like the person that did it just wanted to get the job done.
And they didn't know how to do the little piece in this part on it.
So, I've always been that way.
I'm very mathematically inclined.
I'm not very creative, but, and I think David said he's talking to you or somebody tomorrow when you guys, so yeah, he's my boss.
And so a lot of cloud team does is we maintain basically the portal and all of the hosted infrastructure on our side.
And then, you know, Victor is part of the edge team and they handle the software that goes on the client side.
And then we have the DevOps team, of course, that handles the infrastructure in between.
But, yeah, that's my day-to-day is knee-deep in the weeds in the code, and that's where I'm happy.
Nice, nice.
I guess the other thing that I was curious about was, like, on the integration side, like, is that, like, integration and also, like, future focus?
Like, what are you seeing?
Do you see that a lot of the customers have similar problems when they're trying to implement these systems?
Or is it kind of, like, really...
Really like customer specific.
And then the other thing, well, I'll save it, but I would more future focused stuff.
I'm curious about kind of agentic and stuff like that.
Okay.
So right now there are some very customer specific issues.

I don't necessarily bump that into what I would consider normal customer centric and the fact that it doesn't require a completely new implementation, but it's a very specific twist on something we're already doing.

So, you know, I think that we're covering a lot of the bases, but, you let's say, you know, they're hosting their own GitLab or, you know, they're hosting their own services and they don't want it to be on a public network.
It's got to work on a private network.
You know, there are certain things that we have to do to work around that.
But, you know, by and large, you know, we've been very lucky in the fact that a lot of, especially the early adopters were like, it would be really awesome if you could do this.
And we're willing to work with you until we get there.
So, you know, we're able to build on that and expand it.
you.
Thank you.
Um,
As far as the future goes, I would really like to see us get into the agentic AI train, you know, be able to help support authorization there.

I think there's a big door there, and especially with it being so young, you we might actually be able to get in on some of these focus groups and actually talk to them about how they want to structure that authorization and can kind of help lead that movement or at least be a part of it.

Another thing, and I mean, this has just been personally that I would like to see us do, and I'm going to bring it up because it's in my back burner and I'm pushing it really hard, is I would like to have a developer system set up so that, you know, if a new developer comes on board, you know, instead of like security, having to, you know, create a list of credentials for them and assign them to them and hand them to them, and then they get, you know, they leave and they get fired and you've got to go back and erase all that stuff.
What if you can just assign them their access policy that gives them the exact access you need to all of the components?
And you just add their computer to it, and it's done.
They don't need to have anything.
And then if they leave, because they never had the credentials to begin with, I mean, you obviously should cycle your passwords and stuff.
But I mean, it's no longer a deal about if somebody leaves, all of a sudden now you have a security hole that has to be closed.
Because they never had the credentials in the first place.
You disable that access, it's gone, it's done, it's finished, and everything else continues to run normally.
And I think, especially like even as a developer, I think that would be amazing.
You know, because usually the first week and a half of your job is waiting on the different departments.
Like, okay, I need this access from DevOps, I need this access from security.
I need, you know, just to be able to say, here's your laptop, go do it.
Yeah.
You know, everything I need access to already works.
Yeah, yeah.
So that's kind of the side project that I'm working on right now.
And I'm talking about it with my boss.
And I think that there's going to be a place for that in the future.
I think it sounds great.
I think it sounds like a good even entry product for folks who aren't even necessarily aware that they need the rest of the thing, right?
That's right.
And that's what I told them.
like, I think if we can make this work and keep it solid, this would be one of those door opener products.
Because I mean, I think a lot of people would be really interested in that.
You'd get in the door with that and then be like, what else can you do for me?
And I'm like, this is what else we can do, you know?
So I'm really, you know, I don't know if anybody else will mention that or not, but that's the one that I'm, you know, as a developer, I really want to do that.
think we can really open a space there.

It's, you know, it's just very contained, very secure.
And then once it's gone, it's gone.
And it's it.

Surfaces, like, that feels like one to me.

Like, it's a big funnel opener.
So it sounds great.
And, you know, I told them, we can even stretch it.
It doesn't even have to be developers.

I you could just set it up the same way.

And that's how you could awesome up the maturity scale, right?
Because that would get a lot of your newer companies, and then they'll develop need for the rest of your product as they go on, right?
That's right.

Whereas the smaller customers are like, yeah, I've kind of heard of it, but I haven't really figured out where that fits in my, you know, and I'm like, you know.
This developer or this, you know, end user piece opens that door because everybody could see what that would be useful.
Yeah.
You know, I mean, I don't have to remember 5,000 passwords and all this other stuff.
It just hurts.
I'm like, that's it.
Work.
Yeah, instantly.
Everybody, every part of the organization from the fully non-technical all the way up, right?
That's right.
And then as they grow, we can be like, hey, you're doing this now.
This is what we can, you we can help you there too.
Yeah, because the reality is they will eventually.
Everyone does need it.

It's not about whether they need it or not, right?
But since you've already got that established relationship, you know.
education piece is easier because you've already done it over your years-long conversation relationship.
